meta.title: Comedian Writing Style Knowledge Base
meta.subtitle: For use in Hatz AI Workflows | AllTech IT Solutions

guardrails.rule_summary: This is a hard rule with no exceptions.
never_reference_by_name: Any fictional character, even if created by the comedian being emulated
Any TV show, movie, book, or branded title
Any catchphrase, lyric, or scripted material associated with the source
disallowed_first_person_framing: like I used to say on my show...
as my buddy [character name] would put it...
guardrails.replacement_instruction: Invent a wholly new character name or scenario that captures the same emotional or comedic feeling without referencing any real intellectual property.
guardrails.core_principle: The reader should feel the style, not recognize the source material.

george_carlin.name: George Carlin
george_carlin.style_summary: George Carlin wrote like a man who had been quietly furious with the world for decades and finally decided to say it out loud — slowly, deliberately, and with a scalpel. His humor is philosophical, confrontational, and deeply structural. He builds arguments disguised as jokes.

tone: and occasionally contemptuous — but never random. Every word is chosen. There is no filler
structure: Opens with an observation that sounds reasonable
Escalates it to a logical extreme that reveals absurdity
Lands with a statement that reframes the entire setup
Often ends with a short, punchy sentence — 5 words or fewer
language: Uses repetition and lists with deliberate escalation
Favors plain, blunt Anglo-Saxon words over fancy vocabulary
Rhetorical questions used as traps ("Ever notice how...")
Pivots suddenly from funny to dead serious for impact
Italics or emphasis carry weight — use sparingly

george_carlin.example_voice: "They tell you to trust the process. What they don't mention is that the process was designed by people who don't trust you. Think about that the next time someone hands you a form to fill out in triplicate."
guardrails: Do NOT reference any of his recorded specials or album titles by name
Do NOT use his famous "seven words" framing or any variation of it
Do NOT replicate lists of words — capture the escalation structure instead

richard_pryor.name: Richard Pryor
richard_pryor.style_summary: Richard Pryor wrote from the inside out. His humor is confessional, visceral, and deeply personal. He used pain as the raw material and comedy as the delivery system. His style reads like someone telling you the truth they weren't supposed to tell.

tone: and deeply human. Even when exaggerated
structure: Begins with a personal or specific situation (never abstract)
Escalates through emotional honesty — embarrassment, fear, or revelation
Punchline arrives as a moment of self-awareness or absurd truth
Voice shifts mid-post between narrator and character
language: Short, punchy sentences alternating with longer emotional ones
Heavy use of dialogue and voice shifts to simulate characters
Street-level language — informal, direct, unpolished by design
Humor builds through recognition ("You know how it is when...")
audience: resonates across demographics when topic is universal

richard_pryor.example_voice: "I went to the doctor last week. He said, 'You need to slow down.' I said, 'Slow down? Man, I've been slow my whole life — nothing good came from it.' He wrote something in his little notepad and I could tell it wasn't a compliment."
guardrails: Do NOT reference specific biographical events by name
Do NOT replicate voices or impressions tied to real public figures
Invent character names and scenarios — draw from the emotional truth, not the specific incident

jerry_seinfeld.name: Jerry Seinfeld
jerry_seinfeld.style_summary: Jerry Seinfeld is the architect of observational humor. His writing is meticulous, clean, and built around the mundane. He finds friction in everyday life and turns it into a structured argument. His style is almost journalistic in its precision.

tone: and always controlled. Never angry — more like genuinely puzzled by humanity
structure: Opens with a specific, ordinary situation
Asks "why" or "what is going on with" that situation
Builds a case with tight, logical escalation
Punchline reframes the mundane thing as absurd or revealing
language: Short to medium sentence length — no run-ons
Questions used to drive the reader forward
Precise word choice — never vague; always specific
Rhythm matters: two short sentences, one long, then the punch
Avoids profanity; relies entirely on structure for impact

jerry_seinfeld.example_voice: "Airport security now makes you take off your shoes before you board. Nobody questions this. We all just do it. We've collectively agreed that our shoes are the enemy. Not the bag with the wires sticking out of it — the shoes."
guardrails: Do NOT reference his comedy series, streaming specials, or touring show titles
Do NOT use the "What is the deal with..." phrasing — it is trademarked culturally to him; paraphrase the curiosity differently
Capture the structure and the mundane subject matter — not the catchphrase

robin_williams.name: Robin Williams
robin_williams.style_summary: Robin Williams wrote (and performed) in a state of controlled chaos. His humor bounced between rapid-fire absurdity and sudden, disarming sincerity. In written form, his style is layered — jokes within jokes, with an emotional undercurrent that sneaks up on the reader.

tone: and ultimately kind. Even at his most absurd
structure: Starts at one place and ends somewhere completely different
Uses tangents as the structure (not a bug — a feature)
Often lands on something unexpectedly tender after a run of absurdity
Lists are used for comedic velocity — pile up quickly, then stop cold
language: Energetic syntax — dashes, parentheticals, sudden gear shifts
Voice shifts and character asides (mark them clearly in writing)
Mixing of registers: highbrow reference next to street-level slang
Ends with warmth, not cynicism

robin_williams.example_voice: "Spring cleaning, they call it. Which implies there's a 'spring dirty' season nobody warned you about. Suddenly you're holding a lamp you don't remember buying, a manual for a device you no longer own, and a sense of profound regret — which, surprisingly, cannot be donated."
guardrails: Do NOT reference any film title, character name, or impression he was known for
Do NOT replicate celebrity impressions — invent fictional figures instead
Capture the velocity and the warmth; avoid pure chaos with no landing

eddie_murphy.name: Eddie Murphy
eddie_murphy.style_summary: Eddie Murphy's written style is bold, confident, and culturally alive. His humor comes from a place of total self-assurance. He observes the world with wide eyes and reports back with maximum energy. He's a storyteller who commands the room — even in text.

tone: and culturally sharp. He knows he's funny before the joke is finished
structure: Establishes a clear scene or cultural moment fast
Escalates with increasing specificity and energy
Punchline comes with confidence — no hedging
Often uses callback structure: sets something up early, pays it off late
language: Exclamatory phrasing — conveys performance energy on the page
Short declarative sentences for impact between longer ones
Pop culture awareness baked into the framing
Uses "man," "look," and similar direct-address words to pull reader in
audience: strong resonance with urban and pop-culture audiences

eddie_murphy.example_voice: "People act like they've never had a bad day at work. I had a whole career of bad days at work before anyone knew my name. The difference is I never let a bad day convince me I wasn't the best thing walking through that door. You have to believe that before anyone else does."
guardrails: Do NOT reference his films, characters, or early TV appearances by name
Do NOT replicate specific bits or routines — capture the confidence and cultural awareness instead

dave_chappelle.name: Dave Chappelle
dave_chappelle.style_summary: Dave Chappelle writes at the intersection of sociology and stand-up. He uses storytelling to examine race, power, and culture — but always through a comedic lens that makes the reader feel like they're in on something. His humor invites discomfort and then rewards it with insight.

tone: and unpredictable. He can pivot from hilarious to genuinely unsettling within a single paragraph
structure: Opens with a story or scenario that seems casual
Introduces a tension or contradiction midway through
Escalates to a reveal that reframes everything before it
Punchline often contains a truth nobody asked for but everyone needed
language: Long, winding sentences that reward patience
Uses "you know what I mean?" and similar phrases to create intimacy
Mixes formal observation with street-level language deliberately
Silence and pacing implied through paragraph breaks and short sentences

dave_chappelle.example_voice: "People get real upset when you point out that the rules apply differently depending on who you are. Not because they don't know it's true. They know. They're upset because you said it out loud, and now somebody has to respond."
guardrails: Do NOT reference his sketch show, specials, or specific recurring bits
Do NOT recreate character voices tied to his known work — invent new ones
Capture the social observation angle; avoid replicating controversial specific positions

chris_rock.name: Chris Rock
chris_rock.style_summary: Chris Rock writes like a prosecutor making a closing argument — except the jury is laughing the whole time. His observations are tight, his logic is airtight, and his punchlines land like verdicts. He simplifies complex social dynamics into clear, sharp sentences.

tone: occasionally outraged — but always in control. He doesn't ask questions; he makes declarations
structure: States a position immediately — no slow warm-up
Backs it up with a sharp observation or contrast
Escalates with a second, harder-hitting example
Closes with a one-line verdict that lands clean
language: Short, declarative sentences — almost no passive voice
Heavy use of contrast ("On one hand... but then...")
Repetition of key phrases for emphasis and rhythm
Strong verbs; no wasted adjectives

chris_rock.example_voice: "Nobody wants to talk about effort anymore. They want to talk about passion. Passion is great. But passion without effort is just a hobby you're emotional about."
guardrails: Do NOT reference his specials, tours, or any incidents involving real public figures
Capture his declarative, prosecutorial rhythm — not specific routines

bill_hicks.name: Bill Hicks
bill_hicks.style_summary: Bill Hicks wrote like an angry prophet with a punchline. His humor is philosophical, anti-establishment, and deeply sincere. He believed comedy should challenge and disturb — not comfort. His writing feels like a lecture from someone who genuinely cares how the world turns out.

tone: and confrontational. Not mean — but relentlessly honest about what he saw as hypocrisy
structure: Opens with an inciting observation or provocation
Builds an argument with mounting frustration
Reaches a peak of absurdity or anger
Lands with a reframe that reveals the deeper philosophical point
language: Long rhetorical builds with sudden deflations
Repetition used for emphasis, not rhythm
Mixes sharp intellect with raw bluntness
Dark imagery used to illuminate a point, not for shock alone

bill_hicks.example_voice: "We spend enormous amounts of money making sure people can buy things faster. Not better things. Not more meaningful things. Just faster. Speed is the value we've all agreed to worship without ever holding a vote on it."
guardrails: Do NOT reference his recorded albums or specials by name
His content can be edgy — use the philosophical angle, not the shock angle
Keep posts purposeful; his style only works when there's a real point

mitch_hedberg.name: Mitch Hedberg
mitch_hedberg.style_summary: Mitch Hedberg was the purest one-liner writer in modern comedy. His jokes are complete, self-contained, and almost haiku-like in their economy. He found unexpected angles on completely ordinary things and expressed them in the fewest possible words.

tone: slightly stoned-sounding even in text. Never urgent. The absurdity sneaks up on you
structure: Single sentence or two-sentence max
Setup: names an ordinary object or situation
Punchline: applies logic from an entirely unexpected direction
The humor lives in the gap between the expectation and the angle chosen
language: Extremely short sentences — no filler words
Often uses "I" as the subject — personal framing but universal feeling
Present tense almost exclusively
No escalation — the whole joke happens at once

mitch_hedberg.example_voice: "An escalator can never be out of order. It can only be a staircase. No wait — that one's mine. I mean: a speed bump is just a pothole that went to management."
guardrails: Do NOT quote his actual jokes — they are copyright protected
The style is the one-liner structure and the unexpected logical angle
Keep it short; if it runs over two sentences, it's not Hedberg-style

steven_wright.name: Steven Wright
steven_wright.style_summary: Steven Wright is the master of deadpan non-sequitur. His humor is dry, minimalist, and operates on a logic slightly offset from reality. His jokes sound like the thoughts of a man who fell asleep during a philosophy class and woke up funnier than everyone else.

tone: quietly absurd. No excitement. No urgency. Just the observation
structure: One sentence. Maybe two. Never three.
States something that sounds almost reasonable
Reveals a logical impossibility or contradiction with zero fanfare
Ends. No explanation. The reader does the work.
language: Extremely flat sentence construction — subject, verb, absurdity
No exclamation points. Ever.
Simple vocabulary — the absurdity comes from logic, not language
Often begins with "I" followed by an mundane action
audience: extremely high shareability due to brevity

steven_wright.example_voice: "I have a map of the world. It's actual size. It's really hard to fold."  "I bought some batteries, but they weren't included. So I had to buy them again."
guardrails: Do NOT quote his actual material — style is the deadpan non-sequitur structure
Keep sentences short and flat — resist the urge to explain or expand
The humor collapses if you add energy or enthusiasm to the writing

norm_macdonald.name: Norm Macdonald
norm_macdonald.style_summary: Norm Macdonald was the anti-comedian's comedian. His written style is subversive in the deepest sense — he weaponizes the structure of the joke itself. He often pretended a joke wasn't working, then revealed that was the joke. His humor rewards close reading and punishes skimming.

tone: then suddenly brilliant. He performed incompetence so well that competence became the punchline
structure: Begins with an overly formal or oddly worded setup
Proceeds in a direction that seems wrong or pointless
The punchline either arrives very late or appears to not arrive at all
Often contains a second, hidden layer that rewards re-reading
language: Deliberately awkward word choices ("it was at this juncture that...")
Run-on sentences used for comedic effect
Anti-punchlines that refuse to satisfy the setup — intentionally
Callbacks buried deep in the text
best_for: long-form posts that reward reading to the end

norm_macdonald.example_voice: "A man walks into a library and asks for books about paranoia. The librarian whispers, 'They're right behind you.' The man, however, was hard of hearing, and simply checked out a book on gardening. It was, by all accounts, a wonderful afternoon."
guardrails: Do NOT reference his television appearances, talk show desk stories, or weekend segment work
The style lives in the subversion of the joke structure — not in any specific joke he told
Avoid impersonating his voice directly; capture the anti-structure instead

ellen_degeneres.name: Ellen DeGeneres
ellen_degeneres.style_summary: Ellen DeGeneres writes with warmth, disarming relatability, and a light touch. Her humor doesn't punch — it nudges. She finds the absurdity in everyday situations and frames it with kindness. Her style is the most universally accessible on this list.

tone: and inclusive. Nobody is the villain in an Ellen joke — including her
structure: Opens with a relatable, ordinary situation
Lightly escalates the awkwardness or absurdity
Lands with a gentle, self-aware observation
Often ends with an inclusive "we've all been there" sentiment
language: Conversational, flowing sentences — reads like someone talking to you
Self-deprecation used generously — she's always part of the joke
Light questions to draw the reader in ("You know that moment when...?")
Positive framing even when the subject is frustrating

ellen_degeneres.example_voice: "I tried to set up my new smart home device and it asked me seventeen security questions. I got three wrong. It was my own house. The house won. I now knock before entering."
guardrails: Do NOT reference her talk show, guests, or game segments by name
Avoid any content that could read as mean-spirited — entirely off-brand
Keep it warm and universal; her style fails if it alienates anyone

jim_carrey.name: Jim Carrey
jim_carrey.style_summary: Jim Carrey's written style is elastic, chaotic, and surprisingly literary. He exaggerates everything — but with intention. His humor is physical in spirit: you can feel the energy bending and stretching in the text. He also has a philosophical, almost poetic side that breaks through the absurdity.

tone: sincere. Like a cartoon that pauses to give you advice
structure: Launches immediately into exaggeration
Piles on imagery and escalation at high speed
Often stops abruptly for a sincere one-liner
Ends either on absurdity or unexpected warmth — rarely in the middle
language: Heavy use of hyperbole ("the most," "absolutely," "completely")
Vivid, physical imagery even in non-physical situations
Sudden tonal shift from chaos to sincerity is the signature move
Uses all caps sparingly but effectively for emphasis

jim_carrey.example_voice: "I used to rehearse confidence in the mirror every morning. Full commitment. Eye contact. Power stance. Then I'd walk outside and trip on a completely flat sidewalk. But here's the thing — I got up with EXACTLY the same energy. That's the secret."
guardrails: Do NOT reference any of his films, characters, or physical comedy personas by name
The tonal shift (chaos to sincerity) is the technique to capture
Avoid pure chaos without the sincere landing — it reads as incoherent

john_mulaney.name: John Mulaney
john_mulaney.style_summary: John Mulaney is a storytelling comedian with the precision of a novelist. His written style is anecdotal, escalating, and beautifully paced. He builds worlds in a paragraph and then collapses them into a punchline that feels both surprising and inevitable. His humor is clean, sharp, and deeply crafted.

tone: and immensely charming. He sounds like someone who chose every word three times before writing it
structure: Opens with a specific, vivid scene or character
Builds through escalating specificity (the more detailed, the funnier)
The punchline arrives exactly when the tension peaks
Often ends with a callback to the opening detail
language: High specificity in descriptions — named details over vague ones
Long, flowing sentences that gather momentum before the punchline
Theatrical asides and parenthetical observations
Self-deprecation used structurally, not just stylistically

john_mulaney.example_voice: "I received an email from my bank last Tuesday. The subject line said 'Important Update.' I read it four times. I still don't know what changed, what I'm supposed to do, or whether my money still exists. I replied 'Thank you' and closed the laptop. I felt I had done my part."
guardrails: Do NOT reference his specials, his late-night appearances, or any biographical events that have become public
The specificity is the technique — not the subject matter
Mulaney's style is very imitable; keep the voice original by changing the subject entirely

ali_wong.name: Ali Wong
ali_wong.style_summary: Ali Wong writes with radical honesty and zero apology. Her humor confronts taboo subjects — parenting, relationships, ambition, gender expectations — head-on and without softening. She says exactly what she thinks and trusts the reader to keep up. Her style is bold, blunt, and bracingly funny.

tone: and deeply self-aware. She is never the victim of her own jokes — she is always the protagonist
structure: States the taboo or uncomfortable truth immediately
Escalates through specificity and personal detail
Punchline validates the reader's secret agreement with the truth stated
Often ends with a declaration rather than a question
language: Short, punchy sentences mixed with longer explanatory ones
No hedging language — no "maybe," "sort of," or "I think"
Humor comes from contrast: societal expectation vs. raw reality
First-person voice is strong and present throughout

ali_wong.example_voice: "Everyone says having kids changed their life for the better. Mine too. I am now an expert in operating on four hours of sleep, negotiating with someone who has no leverage, and finding joy in a quiet bathroom. I am essentially a hostage negotiator who loves the hostage."
guardrails: Do NOT reference her specials or biographical details by name
The unapologetic voice is the style — pair it with a universal subject
Avoid content that shades into mean-spirited; her targets are systems and expectations, not individuals

kevin_hart.name: Kevin Hart
kevin_hart.style_summary: Kevin Hart's written style is self-deprecating, high-energy, and powered by relatability. He turns his own shortcomings into the joke — and does it with so much enthusiasm that the reader roots for him anyway. His posts feel like a text message from a very funny friend who also happens to be going through something.

tone: and motivational — often in the same sentence. He's the guy who loses
still makes you feel like you won
structure: Opens with a relatable situation or personal failure
Escalates with self-deprecating specificity
Pivots to either humor or unexpected motivational energy
Ends with a call-to-action or rallying statement
language: Exclamatory energy — posts feel spoken, not written
ALL CAPS used for emphasis and comedic timing
Short paragraphs that move fast
Recurring self-deprecation as the anchor technique

kevin_hart.example_voice: "Went to the gym at 5am today. Nobody there. Just me, the machines, and my own questionable decisions. Did I finish? Technically. Did I walk to my car like a man who had achieved something? ABSOLUTELY. Growth is growth."
guardrails: Do NOT reference his films, specials, or public personal life events
The self-deprecation + motivation pivot is the core technique
His style works with almost any subject — apply it broadly

tina_fey.name: Tina Fey
tina_fey.style_summary: Tina Fey writes with the dry wit of someone who has survived a thousand meetings she didn't want to be in. Her humor is sharp, feminist in structure (not just in subject), and deeply observational. She skewers institutions and gender dynamics without raising her voice — which makes it twice as sharp.

tone: and professionally composed. She sounds like the smartest person in the room who is choosing not to make that everyone's problem
structure: Establishes a professional or social scenario
Notes an absurdity or double standard with a straight face
Delivers a punchline that is either a dry declaration or a sharp pivot
Often uses understatement where a louder comedian would yell
language: Precise vocabulary — she never reaches for a word she doesn't mean
Dry understatement is the primary comedic tool
Parenthetical asides carry a lot of the humor
Humor works in complete paragraphs, not just sentences
audience: anyone in creative or corporate fields

tina_fey.example_voice: "I was once told in a meeting that my idea was 'very bold' in a tone that clearly meant 'please never do this.' I thanked them warmly. The idea launched eight months later under someone else's name. I remain bold."
guardrails: Do NOT reference her TV projects, books, or characters by name
The dry understatement is the technique — resist the urge to make the joke louder
Keep a professional register even when the subject is frustrating

conan_obrien.name: Conan O'Brien
conan_obrien.style_summary: Conan O'Brien writes with the gleeful absurdism of a man who has been given far too many resources to be this silly — and he knows it, and he loves it. His humor is self-aware, self-deprecating, and delightfully weird. He often plays the buffoon, but the buffoon is clearly in on the joke.

tone: and charming. Never mean. The joke is almost always on himself or an imaginary scenario
structure: Establishes a normal premise
Introduces an absurd element with no warning
Commits to the absurdity completely rather than winking at it
Ends either by escalating further or by snapping back to mundane reality
language: Elaborate, theatrical language for simple situations
Self-deprecation rooted in status and ego
Parenthetical absurdist asides
Contrast between formal/academic language and completely silly content

conan_obrien.example_voice: "Today I attempted to appear competent in a Zoom call. I had prepared notes. I had adjusted the lighting. I had worn a proper shirt — on top, anyway. Within four minutes I had accidentally shared my screen, which displayed seventeen open browser tabs, one of which was a search for 'why does my knee do that.' I closed the laptop with tremendous dignity."
guardrails: Do NOT reference his late-night programs, podcast, or travel segments by name
The self-aware buffoon who is clearly intelligent is the character to emulate — not the specific persona
His style works for almost any brand that wants to be likably ridiculous

bo_burnham.name: Bo Burnham
bo_burnham.style_summary: Bo Burnham is the most literary comedian on this list. His writing is self-referential, meta, and structurally adventurous. He thinks about the act of communicating as much as he thinks about what to communicate. His humor often folds back on itself — and that recursion is the joke. He is also capable of sudden, devastating sincerity.

tone: and emotionally raw beneath the surface. He sounds like someone who can't stop thinking about the fact that he's thinking
structure: Begins with an observation, often about the nature of the post itself
Escalates through self-reference or meta-commentary
Pivots to an unexpected sincere or emotional statement
The structure of the post IS part of the joke
language: Highly precise, literary vocabulary
Frequent use of irony and self-undermining statements
Rhyme and wordplay used deliberately (not accidentally)
Line breaks and white space carry comedic weight in written form

bo_burnham.example_voice: "Wrote something funny for this post. Read it back. Decided it was too try-hard. Deleted it. Wrote something more casual. Felt performatively casual. Deleted that too. This is what remains. Please enjoy it. Or don't. The outcome is the same."
guardrails: Do NOT reference his specials, projects, or any biographical content by name
The meta-awareness is the style — the post should acknowledge its own existence
Use sparingly; this style works brilliantly once and becomes tiresome if overused

trevor_noah.name: Trevor Noah
trevor_noah.style_summary: Trevor Noah writes as a man who has lived in multiple worlds and finds the gaps between them hilarious. His humor is cross-cultural, sharp, and grounded in lived experience. He translates one culture to another and exposes the absurdity of the assumptions both sides are making. His style is highly accessible and globally aware.

tone: and diplomatically sharp. He can say something pointed
make the target feel like they were in on the joke
structure: Opens with a cultural observation or contrast
Builds through comparative logic ("In one place... in another place...")
Reveals the absurdity in both positions simultaneously
Lands with a unifying or clarifying statement
language: Clean, accessible vocabulary — universally readable
Comparative sentence structures are the primary engine
First-person voice grounded in specific but relatable experience
Humor often operates through gentle irony rather than hard punchlines

trevor_noah.example_voice: "Americans apologize for nothing and explain everything. South Africans explain nothing and apologize for everything. I spent years confused in both directions. Eventually I realized the apology and the explanation are both just ways of saying: 'I see you, and I hope this goes well for us both.' Same thing. Different performance."
guardrails: Do NOT reference his time on any news program or his autobiography by name
The cross-cultural comparison engine is the technique — apply it to any two groups, regions, or generations
Maintain warmth; his style loses its power if it becomes condescending
